0: <title>Effects of dietary protease on nitrogen emissions from broiler production: a holistic comparison using Life Cycle Assessment </title>
Ilkka Leinonen and Adrian G Williams
1: <header>Abstract </header>

BACKGROUND: The aim of the study was to quantify the effects of the use of a protease Ronozyme' ProAct in broiler feed on the environmental impacts of broiler and broiler feed production chains. This was done by using a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) modelling approach with data from trials using both standard soya-based broiler diets and reduced-protein diets with added protease.

RESULTS: The results for the feed production chain showed that there was a reduction in all environmental impact categories when protease was used in the diets. The biggest reduction occurred in the category of Global Warming Potential, mainly as a result of decreased carbon dioxide emissions from land use changes related to soya production. In the results for the broiler production chain, there were relatively bigger reductions in Eutrophication Potential and especially in Acidification Potential, mainly as a result of reduced feed protein content and subsequent nitrogen emissions from housing and manure management.

CONCLUSION: The use of protease in the broiler diets reduced the environmental impacts of both feed production and broiler production. The latter is mainly through reduced ammonia emissions, which has substantial benefit per se in the poultry industry. © 2015 Society of Chemical Industry

Supporting information may be found in the online version of this article. Keywords: Acidification Potential; ammonia emissions; broiler production; Global Warming Potential; Life Cycle Assessment; protease

3: <header>INTRODUCTION </header>

Agricultural production and especially livestock systems have gen- erally been considered to have various negative environmental impacts, including nutrient leaching and a significant contribution to global warming. Amongst different livestock production sys- items, poultry production has been found to be relatively l'environ- mentally friendly' 2 Despite for example relatively low greenhouse gas emissions, poultry systems have some features that require special attention in terms of their environmental impacts. These particularly include nitrogen emissions, for example in the forms of ammonia emissions to air, nitrous oxide emissions that con- tribute to global warming, and nitrate leaching. These emissions can occur at all stages of the poultry production chain, including growing the feed crops, housing the birds and finally managing the manure. Some can have relatively local effects, e.g. ammonia can have harmful effects close to poultry buildings, while other emissions cause global effects, e.g. nitrous oxide. A systematic, quantitative approach is needed to evaluate the environmental impacts in complex agricultural systems and espe- cially in livestock production where the environmental burdens related to feed production, processing and transport together with the emissions from animal housing and manure manage- ments should be taken into account. A method called Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) has been found to be a suitable approach for

such research problems, as it is designed to account for all bur- dens occurring during the whole production and consumption cycle, starting from raw material extraction through to the dis- posal of end products. 3,4 This method is also able to quantify mul- tiple categories of environmental impacts, e.g. acidification and eutrophication, in addition to the more widely considered 'carbon footprint', which is the only focus of some other environmental impact assessment frameworks. In LCA, impacts are quantified in relation to the functional unit, which is a defined output of the pro- duction system.
The composition of feed has been found to have a major effect on the environmental impacts of broiler production, as it con- tributes both to the emission arising from the feed production

chain and from the broiler housing and subsequent manure management. One suggested method to reduce such emissions is to reduce the required amount of dietary protein (and the nitro- gen content) of the feed by adding a specific enzyme, protease, to the feed, aiming to improve the protein utilisation of the animals.6 The aim of the present study was to quantify the effects of the use of a protease Ronozyme® ProAct in broiler diets on the environ- mental impacts, especially those affected by the nitrogen emis- sions, in the context of standard indoor broiler production in the UK. This was done by using an LCA modelling approach for differ- ent scenarios of broiler production where diets either (a) without protease with standard protein content or (b) with protease com- bined with reduced protein content were applied.

6: The approach taken in the current study was LCA based on systems modelling of agricultural production as described by Williams et al.7-9 and Leinonen et This included both structural mod- els of the industry and mechanistic process models that were uni- fied in the systems approach so that changes in one area caused consistent interactions elsewhere. This approach was applied to both feed crop and animal production. The systems modelled in this study included crop production, non-crop nutrient produc- tion, feed processing, breeding, broiler production (including farm energy and water use and gaseous emissions from housing) and manure and general waste management.

The structural model for the broiler system calculated all of the inputs required to produce the functional unit, taking into account breeding overheads, mortalities and productivity levels. It also calculated the outputs, both useful (broilers) and unwanted (e.g. wastes and mortalities). In the model, changes in the proportion of any activity must result in changes in the proportions of others to keep producing the desired amount of output. How much of each activity was required was established by solving linear equations that described the relationships that linked the activities together.

The model calculated the nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium contents of the manure according to the mass balance principle, i.e. the nutrients retained in the animal body were subtracted from the total amount of nutrients obtained from the feed. For the purpose of the study, it was assumed that all manure was used for soil improvement as a fertiliser.

A separate sub-model for arable production was used to quantify the environmental impacts of the main feed ingredients, with main features as in Williams et al.9 All major crops used for production of broiler feed were modelled. For the crops produced overseas (e.g. soya, maize and sunflower), the production was modelled based as closely as possible on local techniques. Transport burdens for importing overseas crops and burdens from processing the feed were also included.

According to a widely accepted carbon footprinting method, AS 2050,4 the direct greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions resulting from recent land use changes (LUC) for crop production must be included in the carbon footprint (i.e. the sum of the GHG emissions per unit mass of the product) of each crop. Most GHG emissions from LUC are as carbon dioxide (CO2) from loss of soil and biomass carbon. 'Recent LUC' is defined as having occurred during the last 20 years in PAS 2050.4 This time period is arbitrary, given the nonlinear rates of change in soil carbon, but is considered to be a pragmatic value that covers the largest change and the time during which the change is nearly linear. In this study the LUC emissions were mainly related to soya production and arise from

changes from forest or pasture (natural or managed) to arable land. The pasture is mainly cerrado in Brazil and pampa in Argentina, which are the main origin countries of soya used in the UK.¹2 PAS 20504 also states that 'Where the country of production is known, but the former land use is not known, the GHG emissions arising from land use change shall be the estimate of average emissions from the land use change for that crop in that country'. In this study a weighted average for soya was derived from an analysis of land use and crop production statistics of the United Nations Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO). 12 The data were analysed to estimate the rates of change of types of land use and the degree of interchange between crops, taking into account that some soya expansion is actually from existing arable land. This was used to estimate the proportions of soya grown on mature arable land and on that converted from pasture, forest or other land in Brazil and Argentina. LUC emissions were amortised over 20 years for each unit area of land, as in Audsley et al. 13 Annual rates of LUC emissions taken from PAS 20504 were combined with the national yields of soya reported by the FAO to obtain LUC emissions per unit mass of soya. As the overall result of the analysis, the LUC effect of 1.3 kg CO2 was included in the GHG emissions arising from production of 1 kg of soya meal used for broiler feed in the UK.

In order to model the emissions from the manure, the cur- rent study followed the principles of Audsley et al. and Williams al.,7 taking a long-term approach to agriculture, for example ensuring that nitrogen emissions and uptake from manure are accounted for on an infinite time horizon. This differs from the shorter-term methods that are often applied in less mechanis- tic carbon footprinting approaches.4 Poultry manure is a source of direct gaseous emissions of ammonia (NH3), nitrous oxide (N20) and to a lesser extent methane (CH4), which occur during housing, storage and land spreading and were quantified with a separate manure sub-model. Emissions of NH3, N2O and CH4 arising from excreta during housing were calculated following the methods of Williams et al.,7 which are based on UK national inventories.¹5 For example, a nitrogen (N) flow approach is built into the NH3 inventory, so that N ingested must be rigorously parti- tioned through mass balances into N in production and excreted N, which provides the quantities of N to which volatilised N is related. The emissions of N2O and CH4 are 'unofficial' Tier 2 methods in that UK-specific emission factors for emissions from housing are used in this study, but these have not yet been incorporated into the UK GHG inventory for agriculture, which is undergoing major revi- sions (www.ghgplatform.org.uk). Manure management also uses energy, and these burdens were debited against the broilers (along with burdens from direct gaseous emissions). In the model, all of the nutrients applied to the soil as manure were accounted for either as crop products or as losses to the environment. The benefits of plant nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium) remaining in soil after land application were credited to broiler pro- duction by offsetting the need to apply fertiliser, as described by Sandars al. and Williams et al.7

The production system in this study was considered to represent typical UK broiler production as described by Leinonen In such a farm, the total number of birds per house was 28 500 and the stocking density 20.4 birds m-2. Energy consumption at the broiler farms for heating (0.19 liquefied petroleum gas per bird), lighting and ventilation (total electricity use 0.19 kWh per bird) together with information on material consumption such as the type and amount of bedding (0.17 kg wood shaving per bird) were based on average data from typical farms, provided by the UK broiler industry.10 Also the information about broiler breeding

and hatcheries was obtained from the industry. The data on the production of the protease came directly from the manufacturer. Other non-crop feed ingredients were assumed to be analogues of L-lysine, which is provided in diets as a pure amino acid. It is usually manufactured by a fermentation process using Corynebacterium glutamicum. Given that most other biologically based non-crop ingredients are produced in this generic way, the assumption was considered to be reasonable. In addition, the inclusion rate on enzymes is much lower than on pure amino acids. Hence any error introduced by the analogue assumption will be very small indeed. Additional data such as the life cycle inventories of agricultural buildings and machinery came from Williams et al.7 All experimental data on the effects of the dietary protease were provided by the industry or obtained from the literature. The information on the composition of the broiler feed and the performance (including feed consumption) of the birds within each feeding programme came from seven separate trials, the details of some of which were published earlier.20-23 In all these trials, two alternative feeding programmes based either on stan- dard soya-based diets or on low-protein diets with protease sup- plement were applied in broiler production. The protease was added to the feeds based on the guaranteed analysis (75 000 PROT units g-1) of the commercial product. Enzyme prepara- tions (Ronozyme ProAct CT) were obtained from Novozymes A/S (Bagsvaard, Denmark). The protease used in this study was a commercial enzyme produced by submerged fermentation of Bacillus licheniformis containing transcribed genes from Nocar- diopsis prasina. Enzyme activity for this protease is measured in PROT units, with 1 unit defined as the amount of enzyme

that releases 1 umol p-nitroaniline from 1 umol L-1 substrate (Suc-Ala-Ala-Pro-Phe-p-nitroaniline) min-1 at pH 9.0 and 37 °C. An overview of the seven feeding trials is shown in Table 1. Details of the diets (usually three or four successive phase diets during the production cycle) are shown in 'Supporting informa- tion' for trials 1-6 and in Angel et al. for trial 7. The LCA was carried out separately for two alternative systems.

<list>Feed production chain included growing of feed crops, produc- tion of additives, processing of ingredients and mixtures, trans- port of ingredients, production of fertilisers, etc. The system boundary was the feed mill gate and the functional unit 1000 kg broiler feed, calculated on the basis of the relative amount of each phase diet consumed in each trial. The results of all trials 1-7 were applied in this analysis. 
Broiler production chain quantified all impacts related to feed production (as above) and included also energy use in hous- ing the broilers, emissions from the broiler house and storage and land spreading of the manure, broiler breeder production, hatching, etc. The functional unit was 1000 kg expected broiler carcass weight at the farm gate. The expected carcass weight is the body weight excluding feathers, head, neck, feet and inter- nal organs. In this study a constant relationship between the carcass weight and live weight of the bird was assumed, based on data from the UK broiler industry. 10 As trial 7 considered only the starter phase of the bird growth cycle and therefore did not produce finished broilers, it was excluded from this analysis. An overview of the modelling of the broiler production chain, including the use of the data, is shown in Fig. 1.</list>
As an output of the LCA model, the calculated emissions were aggregated into environmentally functional groups. Nitrogen emissions have an important contribution to the following groups.

<list>Global Warming Potential (GWP) is a measure of the GHG emis- sions to the atmosphere and was calculated here using a timescale of 100 years. The main sources of GWP are CO2 from fossil fuel and LUC, N20 and CH4. GWP was quantified in terms of CO2 equivalents: with a 100 year timescale, 1 kg CH4 and N2O are equivalent to 25 and 298 kg CO2 respectively. The sum of GWP per functional unit is also known as the 'carbon footprint'. 
Eutrophication Potential (EP) is used to assess the over-supply (or unnatural fertilisation) of nutrients as a result of nutri- ents reaching water systems by leaching, run-off or atmo- spheric deposition. EP was calculated using the method of the Institute of Environmental Sciences (CML) at Leiden Uni- versity (http://www.leidenuniv.nl/interfac/cml/ssp/index.html).</list>

6: The results for the environmental impacts arising from the feed production chain are presented in Table 2, where in all categories the impacts arising from the protease-supplemented diets are presented as a percentage of the impacts with the soya-based (control) diets. The results show that there is a reduction in all envi- ronmental impact categories, per mass unit of feed, when protease is used in the diets. The main reason for this is the reduction of the amount of soya used in the diets. The biggest reduction occurs in the category of GWP (up to 12% reduction, with an average of 5%). This is mainly caused by decreased CO2 emissions from LUC related to soya production and lower emissions from transport of the soya. With the EP, there were only small differences between the diets, while the AP showed bigger reduction. This resulted from less use of soya, but the reduction is mainly from the transport step, which emits relatively large amounts of SO2 from marine fuels.

Although the environmental impacts of production of the pro- tease were relatively high per unit of product (e.g. GWP 6.5 kg CO2 equivalent kg-1 protease), the amount applied in the broiler diets was so small that the protease inclusion itself had only a minimal effect per unit of broiler feed.

In Table 3 the results for the environmental impacts arising from the whole broiler production chain are presented. Again, in order to make the results comparable, the impacts arising from production with the protease diets are presented as a percentage of the impacts with the control diets. For these results, the absolute values of the impacts can be seen in 'Supporting information'. In the results, there were relatively bigger reductions in EP and especially in AP compared with the feed production chain alone.

Trial|Relative impact (%)|Relative impact (%)|Relative impact (%) Trial|GWP|EP|AP Trial 1|97|100|98 Trial 2|97|98|96 Trial 3|95|100|97 Trial 4|94|99|96 Trial 5|97|100|98 Trial 6|97|99|98 Trial 7|88|96|94 Average|95|99|97

For the AP, the maximum reduction was 9% and the average reduction 5%. The reason for this is that a major part of these impacts arises from the emissions from housing and manure management. When protease was used in the diets, the crude protein content of the feed was reduced, reducing the amount of nitrogen in manure. This reduced the emission of NH3, affecting both the AP and EP, and leaching of nitrate, affecting the EP. NH3 emissions from housing and manure were the biggest single source of emissions contributing to the AP in the broiler production chain. In general, NH3 emissions are considered to be problematic in poultry production and a subject of regulation in the case of large broiler production units. Therefore a substantial benefit of reducing the protein content of the diet is the reduction of NH3 emissions per se. This is presented in Table 4, which shows the absolute values of the NH3 emissions to air (kg NH3 per 1000 kg broiler carcass at farm gate) for each of the trials considered in this study, both for the standard soya-based diet and for the alternative low-protein diet with protease supplementation.

9: The results for the environmental impacts arising from the feed production chain are presented in Table 2, where in all categories the impacts arising from the protease-supplemented diets are presented as a percentage of the impacts with the soya-based (control) diets. The results show that there is a reduction in all envi- ronmental impact categories, per mass unit of feed, when protease is used in the diets. The main reason for this is the reduction of the amount of soya used in the diets. The biggest reduction occurs in the category of GWP (up to 12% reduction, with an average of 5%). This is mainly caused by decreased CO2 emissions from LUC related to soya production and lower emissions from transport of the soya. With the EP, there were only small differences between the diets, while the AP showed bigger reduction. This resulted from less use of soya, but the reduction is mainly from the transport step, which emits relatively large amounts of SO2 from marine fuels.

Although the environmental impacts of production of the pro- tease were relatively high per unit of product (e.g. GWP 6.5 kg CO2 equivalent kg-1 protease), the amount applied in the broiler diets was so small that the protease inclusion itself had only a minimal effect per unit of broiler feed.

In Table 3 the results for the environmental impacts arising from the whole broiler production chain are presented. Again, in order to make the results comparable, the impacts arising from production with the protease diets are presented as a percentage of the impacts with the control diets. For these results, the absolute values of the impacts can be seen in 'Supporting information'. In the results, there were relatively bigger reductions in EP and especially in AP compared with the feed production chain alone.

as not containing the whole production cycle, the results of this trial are not directly comparable with the other studies. A large part of the reduction in GWP observed in this study is a result of reduced GHG emissions arising from LUC. These emissions should be allocated to production of certain crops, including South American soya, unless the exact origin of the crop is known and certified to be 'mature' agricultural land, as specified in PAS 2050.4 However, it should be noted that currently there is no single generally accepted method how to account for the GHG emissions related to LUC. In this study a method recommended by PAS 2050 was used, but alternative methods could produce strongly differing results when different diets containing varying amounts of soya are compared. For example, it should be noted that there is a more recent version of the PAS 2050 specification²6 where in some cases it is recommended that, from two optional accounting methods, the one producing the 'highest calculated value' of GHG emissions should be used in the assessment. This approach was not used in this study because it was considered that systematically selecting a higher value from two options would violate the Completeness and Consistency principles of the PAS 2050 guidelines and lead to overestimation of the emissions, as discussed by Leinonen al.2 Also, the calculations of the environmental impacts of soya meal were based on the current mixtures of sources of soya bean and soya meal in the UK. If more soya was sourced from Brazil rather than Argentina, this would increase the overall GWP of the soy meal, and this would also increase the relative reduction of this impact when protease is applied in the diet. Amongst the other environmental impacts associated with the feed production, in the EP, there were only small differences between the diets, while the AP showed bigger reduction, again as a result of lower requirements of soya. Amongst all the feeding programmes analysed, the biggest reductions occurred in the study by Angel et al. ² where more soya is removed from the diets than in the other studies. In the results of the analysis of the whole broiler production chain (Table 3 for the GWP, the reductions in environmental impacts were smaller than in the case of the feed production chain. The reason for this is that in broiler production there are GHG emissions arising from other sources than feed and manure (where the emissions are affected by the nitrogen content of the diet). The additional sources include for example the farm fuel and electricity use, and these are not expected to be affected by the differences in the diets. In contrast, there were relatively bigger reductions in EP and especially in AP in the overall broiler production compared with the feed production chain alone. The reason for this is that a major part of these impacts arises from the emissions from housing and manure management. When protease was used in the diets, the crude protein content of the feed was reduced, which automatically reduces the amount of nitrogen excreted by the birds even if the nitrogen retention remains constant. This effect reduced the emission of NH3 (presented in Table which affects both the AP and EP, and leaching of nitrate, affecting the EP, and also has indirect effects on the GWP by contributing to the emission of N2O. In general, the use of protease systematically reduced all envi- ronmental impacts considered in most of the trials analysed in this study. The only exception was trial 6, where the birds on the low-protein diet had a worse performance (higher feed consumption per unit live weight and higher mortality) than the control birds (details of the bird performance in all trials are shown|the respect that rapeseed meal (and to a lesser extent sunflower meal) was used as a major protein source in addition to soya bean meal, which was the main protein source in the other experiments. Whether the effect on performance was related to the enzyme specificity for the protein structure per se of these materials or rather the protein in such materials is simply less accessible to the protease owing to the complex cell wall structure is unclear. How- ever, it should also be noted that in this trial the reduction in amino acid content in the low-protein diet was larger than in some oth- ers. This may also have had an effect on the bird performance. As a result of reduced performance with the low-protein diet in this trial, the impacts in all categories were higher with the protease diet compared with the control treatment. This demonstrates the fact that although the reduction of soya has potential to reduce the environmental impacts, it should be done in a way that has no neg- ative effect on bird performance, otherwise the beneficial effects of such diet changes can be easily lost. In order to compare statistically the environmental impacts for example between different production methods, the uncertainty related to the estimates of the impact categories should also be taken into account. In earlier LCA studies on poultry production the uncertainty has been estimated using Monte Carlo simulations10.1 or with analytical error propagation.² These approaches would require quantitative estimates of the uncertainties of the model inputs, which were not available for all of the data applied in this study, so any formal uncertainty analysis was not carried out. However, it should be noted that the uncertainty analysis should focus on those model variables whose uncertainty is expected to vary between the systems under consideration, i.e. the 'alpha' uncertainties. In all of the trials analysed in this study, both treatments were carried out in a similar environment, so differ- ences could be expected only in the feed composition and in the animal performance. The feed composition was well specified, so minimal uncertainty was expected to occur in this quantity. Fur- thermore, the origin of the feed ingredients was the same for both treatments, with only their relative amounts varying. As a result, the main findings of the environmental benefits of protease can be expected to contain only minimal uncertainty when the feed production chain is considered. In the broiler production chain the differences in bird performance remain the only potential major source of 'alpha' uncertainty. It can therefore be concluded that the predicted environmental benefits with protease will be achieved with high certainty unless there are any negative effects on animal performance. In most of the original trials whose data were used in this study, such effects were not observed. The magnitude of the reduction in environmental impacts in this study was similar or higher compared with other studies aiming to reduce the soya in broiler diets. For example, Leinonen al.5 found that when using 'realistic' inclusion rates of peas replacing part of soya as a protein source in broiler diets, the GWP of broiler production could be reduced by about 4%, i.e. with a similar mag- nitude as observed in many trials analysed in this study. With other European protein sources (beans, sunflower) in diets, the reduc- tion was smaller or did not exist. However, it should be noted that in those studies the performance of the birds was assumed to remain unchanged; if in reality the alternative protein crops have negative effects on e.g. growth, the environmental benefits can be expected to be even smaller. Similarly, other studies have also found a limited potential of alternative protein crops in reduc- tion of the impacts of livestock production.28,29 This indicates that when aiming to improve the environmental performance of

Amongst different methods aiming to reduce the NH3 emissions arising from poultry production, a nutritional solution can be considered to be the most practical as it requires no changing in building design or use of retrofitted scrubbers. Reduced NH3 emissions should also improve air quality for both birds and workers. In broiler production the main motivation for using protease is reduction of feeding costs, as protein is one of the most expensive components in diets. The results of this study suggest that with protease supplement, as long as there are no negative effects on bird performance, this economic benefit can also be combined with environmental improvements.
